---
format:
  revealjs:
    scrollable: true
    self-contained: true
    slide-number: "c/t"
    incremental: false
    reference-location: document
    progress: true
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    logo: "images/kelley-marketing-revised-removebg-preview.png"
    footer: ""
    theme: ["simple", "styles.scss"]
    code-link: true
    multiplex: false
execute:
  echo: false
editor:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

# {data-menu-title="Title Slide" style="text-align: left;" data-background-color="#FFFFFF"}

::: {style="margin-left: 20px; margin-top: 20px;"}

<h1 style="font-size: 2.75em; margin-top: 0; line-height: 1.1;">
Experiential Learning<br>
<span style="font-weight: lighter; color: #555;">& Gender Stereotypes in Science Education</span>
</h1>

<div style="font-size: 0.75em; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 4px; color: #990000; margin-top: 20px; font-weight: 600;">
Evidence from a Field Intervention and Two Experiments
</div>

<br>

<div style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #333; border-left: 3px solid #990000; padding-left: 15px;">
**2026 ISMS Marketing Science Conference**
</div>

<br>

**Muzeeb Shaik**
<div style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #990000; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Kelley School of Business | Indiana University, Bloomington
</div>

<div style="font-size: 0.7em; color: #888; margin-bottom: 40px;">
**With:** Huanhuan Shi, Adithya Pattabhiramaiah, Hari Sridhar
</div>

:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Women remain underrepresented in STEM: a persistent gap across all economic contexts {.smaller}

::::::::::: columns
:::::: {.column width="50%"}

### Global Picture

- Women: only **35%** of STEM graduates worldwide
- In the US: **38.8%** of science bachelor's degrees
- Only **26%** of the US STEM workforce
- **Stakes:** substantial loss of productive capacity

::::::

:::::: {.column width="50%"}

### India: Our Study Context

- Women: **43%** of total undergraduate enrollment
- But only **29%** in STEM disciplines
- Fewer than **18%** of scientists/engineers in labor force
- **Opportunity:** gender parity could add **10-12 million** STEM workers

::::::
:::::::::::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Today's Talk
What if we could address gender stereotypes **indirectly**, by **redesigning the learning experience itself**?
:::

## Three categories of prior interventions, and their common limitation {.smaller}

::::::::: columns
::::: {.column width="33%"}

### 1. Push Campaigns

- Awareness campaigns
- Mass communication
- Public service announcements
- Media messaging

**Goal:** persuade individuals to reconsider stereotypes

*(Paluck et al. 2021; Hermann et al. 2022)*

:::::

::::: {.column width="33%"}

### 2. Girls-Only Programs

- After-school STEM activities
- Mentoring programs
- Interactions with STEM professionals
- Separation as confidence-building

**Goal:** close information gaps, build exposure

*(Dubetz & Wilson 2013; Levine et al. 2015)*

:::::

::::: {.column width="33%"}

### 3. Gender-Congruent Environments

- Female role models
- Feminine classroom décor
- Identity-affirming cues

**Goal:** counteract masculine coding of science

*(Master et al. 2016; Breda et al. 2023)*

:::::
:::::::::

::: {.callout-warning}
## The common limitation
All three **explicitly position gender as the focal problem** and address it through direct persuasion or identity-targeted messaging, which can trigger **reactance**. Crucially, **none engage boys**, the group that holds stronger stereotypes and whose perceptions shape whether girls feel they belong in science.
:::

## Our approach: indirect change through equal-status contact {.smaller}

:::::::::: columns
::::: {.column width="50%"}

### Mechanism

We change **what students observe**, not what they are told.

- Hands-on science in **mixed-gender, equal-status teams**
- Girls and boys do the same tasks, side by side
- Stereotypes are challenged by **firsthand evidence**, not assertion

:::::

::::: {.column width="50%"}

### Theoretical anchor

**Intergroup contact theory** (Allport): contact reduces bias when it is

- equal-status,
- cooperative toward a shared goal,
- institutionally sanctioned.

The science lab supplies all three as a **byproduct of good pedagogy**.

:::::
::::::::::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Core insight
Belief change is a **side effect** of the learning experience, which is what makes it credible and durable.
:::

## Setting: Mobile Science Labs in Bodoland, Assam {.smaller}

:::::::::: columns
::::: {.column width="50%"}

### The intervention

- **Mobile Science Labs (MSL)** visit rural public schools
- **Eight 2-hour** hands-on sessions over one academic year
- Curriculum built on **Kolb's experiential learning cycle**
- Students work in **mixed-gender teams** on equipment they otherwise never access

:::::

::::: {.column width="50%"}

<div style="display: flex; flex-direction: column; align-items: center; gap: 8px;">

<div style="text-align: center; width: 100%;">
<img src="images/msl_vehicle_1.jpeg" style="width: 75%; border-radius: 4px;"/>
<div style="font-size: 0.75em; color: #555; margin-top: 4px;">Mobile Science Lab Vehicle</div>
</div>

<div style="display: flex; gap: 6px; width: 75%; justify-content: center;">
<img src="images/hands_on_learning_1.jpeg" style="width: 49%; height: auto; object-fit: contain; border-radius: 4px;"/>
<img src="images/hands_on_learning_2.jpeg" style="width: 49%; height: auto; object-fit: contain; border-radius: 4px;"/>
</div>

</div>

:::::
::::::::::

::: {.callout-note}
## Why this is a clean test
Baseline classrooms are lecture-only with few labs; MSL introduces **equal-status collaboration where none existed before**, with the same facilitators and curriculum across schools. Gender is **never** the topic, only the unspoken structure of the activity.
:::

## Research Design {.smaller}

:::::::::: columns
::::: {.column width="50%"}

### Sample

- **N = 5,216 students**, **196 public schools**, Grades 6-8
- Treatment: **4,608** students received MSL
- Control: **608** students, regular instruction
- **Two-wave panel**: baseline and endline within the year

:::::

::::: {.column width="50%"}

### Estimation

Difference-in-differences with fixed effects:

$Y_{ist} = \beta_1 Trt_s + \beta_2 Post_t + \beta_3 (Trt_s \times Post_t) + \alpha_{s} + \varepsilon_{ist}$

- $\beta_3$ (Treatment $\times$ Post) is the **causal effect**
- School and grade fixed effects
- **SEs clustered at the school level**

:::::
::::::::::

::: {.callout-note}
## Two primary outcomes
**Science knowledge** (% of grade-aligned items correct) and **gender-stereotype attitude** ("Science is for boys more than girls," 1-5).
:::

## Model-Free Evidence {.smaller}

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Science Knowledge
![](images/Science%20Knowledge%20Score%20Bodoland.png){width="92%"}

::: callout-note
**Catch-up:** treatment starts lower (16%) but overtakes control (29%) by endline.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Gender Stereotypes
![](images/Gender%20Stereotype%20Attitudes%20Bodoland.png){width="92%"}

::: callout-note
Control attitudes stay stable; treatment lowers "science is for boys" beliefs.
:::
:::
:::

## Main Results: Difference-in-Differences {.smaller}

:::: columns

::: {.column width="72%"}

::: {style="font-size: 0.72em;"}
<p style="text-align: center; font-weight: bold; color: #990000;">Panel A: Science Knowledge Score (0-100)</p>

|  | No FE | School FE | School + Grade FE | School × Grade FE |
|------|------|------|------|------|
| [**Treatment × Post**]{.my-crimson} | 13.763*** | 13.763*** | 13.763*** | 13.763*** |
|  | (2.743) | (2.743) | (2.747) | (2.743) |
| N | 10,432 | 10,432 | 10,432 | 10,432 |
| R² | .076 | .255 | .317 | .398 |

<p style="text-align: center; font-weight: bold; color: #990000;">Panel B: Gender-Stereotype Attitudes (1-5)</p>

|  | No FE | School FE | School + Grade FE | School × Grade FE |
|------|------|------|------|------|
| [**Treatment × Post**]{.my-crimson} | -0.595** | -0.576** | -0.575** | -0.573** |
|  | (0.186) | (0.188) | (0.188) | (0.188) |
| N | 10,214 | 10,214 | 10,214 | 10,214 |
| R² | .061 | .205 | .207 | .236 |

<p style="text-align: center;">*SEs clustered at school level. *** p<.001, ** p<.01*</p>
:::
:::

::: {.column width="28%"}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Knowledge
**+13.8 points**

≈ **88%** over baseline; stable across all specifications
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Stereotypes
**-0.58 points**

≈ **20%** reduction; robust to all controls
:::

:::

::::

## Heterogeneity and Broader Spillovers {.smaller}

:::::::::: columns
::::: {.column width="50%"}

### Who updates? (moderation by gender)

- Knowledge gains are **equal** for boys and girls (Trt×Post×Male: 0.642, p = .836)
- Stereotype reduction is **~3× larger for boys** (Trt×Post×Male: -0.658, p = .002)

::: {.callout-note}
Boys hold **higher baseline bias**, so equal-status contact gives them the most to update. Consistent with contact theory.
:::

:::::

::::: {.column width="50%"}

### Effects extend beyond the two primary outcomes

- Positive effects on **12 of 14** science-attitude constructs (interest, self-efficacy, orientation, value)
- **Perceptions of excellence:** treated students less likely to assume a top student is a boy
  - Science domain: **β = 0.55***
  - Language arts: **β = 0.45***

:::::
::::::::::

## But the field bundles many features {.smaller}

:::::::::: columns
::::: {.column width="55%"}

The MSL program combines several things at once:

- Experiential, hands-on pedagogy
- **Mixed-gender** collaborative grouping
- Equal-status peer interaction
- Extended exposure over several sessions

The field data **cannot isolate** whether stereotype reduction comes from the gender composition of groups or simply from hands-on learning.

:::::

::::: {.column width="45%"}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Two randomized studies
**1. Scenario experiment** (N = 151 US adults, Prolific): vignettes that manipulate the gender composition of a science task.

**2. Synthetic replication** (450 LLM agents): does the pattern hold under a very different participant source?
:::

:::::
::::::::::

## Study 2: Scenario Experiment {.smaller}

:::::::::: columns
::::: {.column width="48%"}

### Design

- Between-subjects, **3 conditions**: Mixed-Gender (Emily & Jake), Solo-Male, Solo-Female
- Comic-strip vignettes of the **same** chemistry experiment; differ **only** in gender composition
- **N = 151** US adults 18-30; 100% passed attention checks
- Outcome: 4-item stereotype composite (α = .845)

:::::

::::: {.column width="52%"}
![](images/scenario_experiment_bar.png){width="88%"}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Contact beyond role models
Among **female participants**, Mixed-Gender (M = 1.31) scores **below** Solo-Female (M = 1.49): contact reduces stereotypes beyond a counter-stereotypical exemplar (d = -0.63, p = .021).
:::

:::::
::::::::::

## Study 3: Synthetic Participant Replication {.smaller}

:::::::::: columns
::::: {.column width="48%"}

### Setup

- **450 LLM agent surrogates** (150 per condition), Claude Sonnet API
- Personas vary in age, gender, race, major, and science attitudes
- Text versions of the same three vignettes; **projective** item framing
- Builds on the *homo silicus* framework (Horton 2023)

:::::

::::: {.column width="52%"}
![](images/edsl_synthetic_bar.png){width="88%"}

::: {.callout-tip}
## Same pattern, different source
Mixed-Gender < Solo-Male replicates (d = 0.65, p < .001). A **directional robustness check**, not independent causal evidence: agents may reflect training-data regularities.
:::

:::::
::::::::::

## Contribution & Takeaway {.smaller}

:::::::::: columns
::::: {.column width="50%"}

### What we add

- A **pedagogy-first** intervention shifts stereotypes with **no gender messaging**
- Causal field evidence at scale (**5,216 students, 196 schools**), triangulated by **two experiments**
- Extends **shared consumption** and **social learning** from commercial to educational settings: belief change as experience, not persuasion

:::::

::::: {.column width="50%"}

### Why it matters

- Effects are broad (12 of 14 attitudes) and reach **perceptions of who belongs** in science
- Boys, who hold the strongest priors, update the most: **key agents of change**
- Equal-status contact, not just exposure to exemplars, is the active ingredient

:::::
::::::::::

::: {.callout-important}
## Bottom line
Designing the **experience** well, not delivering the **message**, is what shifts both learning and stereotypes.
:::

## Thank You {.center data-background-color="#FFFFFF"}

<div style="font-size: 1.5em; color: #555; margin-top: 20px;">
Questions & Comments?
</div>

<hr style="border-top: 3px solid #990000; width: 100px; margin: 20px 0;">

<div style="font-size: 1.2em; font-weight: bold;">
Muzeeb Shaik
</div>
<div style="font-size: 0.8em; color: gray;">
Assistant Professor</div>
<div style="font-size: 0.8em; color: gray;">
Kelley School of Business</div>
<div style="font-size: 0.8em; color: gray;">
Indiana University - Bloomington</div>
<div style="margin-top: 10px;">
<a href="mailto:shaikmu@iu.edu" style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #990000; text-decoration: none; font-weight: bold;">shaikmu@iu.edu</a>
</div>

# Appendix {visibility="uncounted"}

## Additional Results: Robustness Check (CEM Matching) {.smaller visibility="uncounted"}

**Addressing baseline differences in science knowledge between treatment and control.**

:::::: columns

::: {.column width="50%"}

**Pre-match balance**

- Treatment: 15.87%
- Control: 26.27%
- Std. mean diff: -0.64

**Post-match balance**

- Both: 15.87%
- Std. mean diff: 0.00
- Balance achieved

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**CEM-weighted results**

- Treatment × Post: **4.62†**
- (SE = 2.37, p = .053)
- Confirms robustness

✓ Knowledge effect holds after controlling for baseline proficiency; the stereotype effect was never confounded by a pre-period gap.

:::

::::::

## Additional Results: Primary Outcome Measures {.smaller visibility="uncounted"}

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Gender-stereotype attitude

Single-item, baseline and endline:

> **"I feel science is for boys more than girls."**

- 5-point Likert (1 = Strongly Disagree, 5 = Strongly Agree)
- Higher scores = stronger endorsement
- N = 10,214 with complete two-wave responses

*A concrete, single-referent belief, for which single-item measurement is appropriate (Bergkvist & Rossiter 2007).*

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### Science knowledge score

- Standardized, grade-appropriate test
- Chemistry, physics, biology aligned with the state curriculum
- Scored as percentage correct
- N = 10,432 with complete two-wave responses
- Administered at baseline and endline

:::
:::

## Additional Results: Broader Science Attitudes (14 items) {.smaller visibility="uncounted"}

### Four conceptual categories

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**1. Interest & enjoyment**

- I enjoy doing science activities
- Science is one of my favorite subjects
- I look forward to science class
- I find science interesting

**2. Self-efficacy**

- I feel confident solving science problems
- I can understand difficult science concepts
- I am good at science

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

**3. Career intentions in STEM**

- I want a career that uses science
- I would like to be a scientist / engineer / doctor
- I can picture myself working in a science field

**4. Perceived social value**

- Science helps solve important problems
- My community values science
- Science is useful in everyday life
- Understanding science helps me make good decisions

*All items: 5-pt Likert.*

:::
:::

## Additional Results: Gendered Perception of Excellence {.smaller visibility="uncounted"}

### Projective post-intervention item

For each of two domains, respondents were asked:

> **"A student performs very well in [science / language arts]. Is this student a boy or a girl?"**

- Response options: **Boy** / **Girl** / **Could be either**
- Administered separately for **science** and **language arts** excellence
- Coded to capture attributions made **more equally across genders**
- The language-arts version is a **placebo check**: does the intervention shift attributions only in the targeted domain?

## Additional Results: Scenario Experiment (Composite) {.smaller visibility="uncounted"}

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Four items (5-pt Likert; α = .845)

1. *"Boys are naturally better at science than girls."*
2. *"Science is more of a 'boy thing' than a 'girl thing.'"*
3. *"Girls can be just as good at science as boys."* **(reverse-coded)**
4. *"Boys tend to be more interested in science than girls."*

Item 3 reverse-coded, then all four averaged; higher composite = stronger endorsement.

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### Sample & procedure

- **N = 151** adults aged 18-30 on **Prolific**
- US residents, at least secondary education
- 59.6% female, 37.1% male, 3.3% non-binary
- Approximately balanced across 3 conditions (n ≈ 50 each)
- Viewed one vignette, then two attention checks before the stereotype items

:::
:::

## Additional Results: Synthetic Replication (Agents) {.smaller visibility="uncounted"}

::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

### Persona dimensions (each agent unique)

- **Age** (18-30, matching the Prolific sample)
- **Gender** (balanced across male, female, non-binary)
- **Race/ethnicity** (US census-matched)
- **Academic major** (STEM / non-STEM / undeclared)
- **Baseline science attitudes** (low / moderate / high)

### Stimuli

- **Text-based** versions of the same three conditions
- 150 agents per condition (N = 450)

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### Execution (Blanchard et al. 2025)

- **Model:** Claude Sonnet via Anthropic API
- Fixed model version, controlled temperature
- Complete response logging, including agent reasoning

### Item framing vs. human study

- **Projective framing** (third-person; Luchs et al. 2010) to reduce social-desirability bias
- Item content otherwise mirrors the human experiment

:::
:::

## Vignette Stimuli: The Three Conditions {.smaller visibility="uncounted"}

<div style="display: flex; justify-content: center; gap: 18px; margin-top: 10px;">

<div style="width: 480px; text-align: center;">
<div style="font-weight: bold; color: #990000; margin-bottom: 6px; font-size: 0.95em;">Mixed-Gender (Emily & Jake)</div>
<img src="images/scenario_stimulus_mixed_gender.png" width="480" style="display: block; width: 480px; height: auto; border: 1px solid #ccc; border-radius: 6px;"/>
</div>

<div style="width: 480px; text-align: center;">
<div style="font-weight: bold; color: #990000; margin-bottom: 6px; font-size: 0.95em;">Solo-Male (Jake)</div>
<img src="images/scenario_stimulus_solo_male.png" width="480" style="display: block; width: 480px; height: auto; border: 1px solid #ccc; border-radius: 6px;"/>
</div>

<div style="width: 480px; text-align: center;">
<div style="font-weight: bold; color: #990000; margin-bottom: 6px; font-size: 0.95em;">Solo-Female (Emily)</div>
<img src="images/scenario_stimulus_solo_female.png" width="480" style="display: block; width: 480px; height: auto; border: 1px solid #ccc; border-radius: 6px;"/>
</div>

</div>

<div style="font-size: 0.8em; color: #555; margin-top: 14px; text-align: center; max-width: 1200px; margin-left: auto; margin-right: auto;">
All three vignettes depict the same chemistry experiment (temperature effects on reaction rate) with identical dialogue content; only the gender composition of the protagonists varies.
</div>
